1.pericope_label: High Priests and the Son of God
1.exposition: The writer has already named Jesus a “great high priest” (4:14); now he pauses to define what a priest is, and then to lay each mark of the office against Christ. A high priest is taken from among men and appointed to act on their behalf in the things of God, offering gifts and sacrifices for sins; he must be able to deal gently with the ignorant and wayward, being himself beset with weakness, and so must offer for his own sins as well (v.3); and he does not seize the honour but is called by God, as Aaron was. Then the criteria are turned on Christ. He did not exalt himself but was appointed by the Father who said, “You are my Son” (v.5), and “You are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek” (v.6); in the garden he offered prayers with loud cries and tears and was heard; and though a Son, he learned obedience through what he suffered, so that, made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation, designated a high priest after Melchizedek’s order. The remarkable result is that Christ meets each qualification yet surpasses it: appointed by an oath and not merely a command, sinless and needing no offering for himself, perfected through obedience unto death rather than through repeated ritual.
1.original_language: Four terms carry the section. In v.2 the priest can metriopathein (“deal gently, feel with moderation”) — a philosopher’s word for bridled feeling, neither cold apathy nor angry severity. In v.7 he “was heard” for his eulabeia (“godly fear, reverent submission”), not for any privilege of rank. In v.8 comes the paronomasia emathen aph’ hōn epathen (“he learned from the things he suffered”). And in v.9 he is teleiōtheis (“having been made perfect” — better, consecrated), the word Calvin ties to his very installation as priest.
1.historical_context: Two features of the ancient office stand behind the argument. On the Day of Atonement the high priest offered first a sin-offering for himself and his house before he could act for the people (Leviticus 16) — a weakness Christ did not share, offering only for others. And the pairing of Psalm 2:7 with Psalm 110:4 joins the royal and priestly messianic hopes the Old Testament largely kept apart — at Qumran two messiahs were expected, one kingly and one priestly, while Hebrews finds both fulfilled in one. The “loud cries and tears” point unmistakably to Gethsemane (Mark 14:32).
1.christ: Every criterion of the office becomes a window on the Son. He is the priest the Father appointed by name and by oath, uniting in himself the King of Psalm 2 and the priest of Psalm 110. He is the priest who wept and pleaded in the garden, so that no sufferer ever comes to a stranger. And he is the priest perfected through obedience unto death, who therefore does not merely point to salvation but is its very source — the author of an eternal deliverance for all who obey him.

verses.note: The writer defines the office before he fills it: a high priest is taken from among men and appointed on their behalf in the things of God, to offer gifts and sacrifices for sins. eastern Chrysostom notes that Paul opens the whole comparison from the priesthood itself, for the new covenant had no temple, no Holy of Holies, no priest in splendid apparel to display — its glories were spiritual and had to be proved from first principles. reformed Calvin adds why the priest must be a man: since we stand far from God, we come before him only in the person of one who is himself one of us, and who never approaches empty-handed but with a sacrifice to atone.

A needful qualification: the priest can deal gently with the ignorant and the wayward, since he himself is beset with weakness. grammatical-historical Robertson recovers the rare word metriopathein — a philosophical term for measured feeling, set against the Stoic’s cold apatheia: a bridled patience that neither rages at the sinner nor grows indifferent to him. grammatical-historical Clarke hears a mercy proportioned to each man’s ignorance and frailty, slow to punish and prone to pardon. The objects are the ignorant and those out of the way — sins of ignorance and straying, not the high-handed sin for which the law made no provision.

By reason of that weakness, he must offer for his own sins as well as the people’s. grammatical-historical Barnes presses the point: the earthly priest, being himself a sinner and imperfect, needs the very atonement he administers. grammatical-historical Ellicott recalls the ritual of the Day of Atonement (Leviticus 16), where the high priest confessed and offered first for himself and his house before he could act for the congregation. Here lies a real inferiority of Aaron’s line — one that will throw the sinless Christ, who offered only for others, into bright relief.

The second requisite: no one takes this honour to himself, but only he who is called by God, as Aaron was. grammatical-historical Gill marks the priesthood a high and sacred dignity that none may seize or intrude upon. reformed Calvin draws the larger rule: what makes any office in the Church lawful is the call of God, not the will of men — no one may thrust himself in, and no ministry may be invented that God has not commanded. The lawful priest waits to be appointed.

Now the criteria are laid against Christ, beginning with the call: he did not glorify himself to become a high priest, but was appointed by the Father who said, “You are my Son; today I have begotten you” (Psalm 2:7). grammatical-historical Barnes notes that Jesus did not intrude into the office but received it by direct designation. grammatical-historical Gill observes that to be made priest was itself a glorifying of the Son — not as God, to whose glory nothing can be added, but as man, whose nature was honoured in the appointment. puritan Matthew Henry sharpens it: “God never said thus to Aaron” — the Son’s call rests on a dignity no Levite ever knew.

A second oracle seals the appointment and lifts it above Aaron: “You are a priest forever, after the order of Melchizedek” (Psalm 110:4). grammatical-historical Barnes marks the two great notes — forever, a priesthood that never passes to a successor, and after the order of Melchizedek, a royal and abiding kind, not the changing, dying line of Levi. puritan Matthew Henry contrasts them plainly: Aaron’s priesthood was temporary and successive, handed from father to son; Christ’s is perpetual and personal, held by one who abides a priest continually — a theme the writer reserves for chapter 7.

The high priest must feel for the weak, and Christ proved he could: in the days of his flesh he offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and was heard for his godly fear. grammatical-historical Gill takes “the days of his flesh” as the whole time of his humiliation, when he bore the sinless infirmities of our nature; grammatical-historical Barnes fixes the scene in Gethsemane (Matthew 26:36), the “strong crying” the outcry of a soul in agony (cf. Luke 22:44). puritan Matthew Henry answers the puzzle of how he “was heard”: not by escaping death but by being supported under it and carried through it — “there is no real deliverance from death but to be carried well through it.”

The wonder of the incarnation in a single line: though he was a Son, he learned obedience from what he suffered. grammatical-historical Robertson hears the Greek wordplay — emathen aph’ hōn epathen, “he learned from the things he suffered” — and insists it means a genuine growth in the experience of obedience, not that he was ever disobedient. reformed Calvin explains: by denying his own dread and drinking the cup, he came to know what full submission to the Father costs, leaving us an example. wesleyan-arminian Wesley notices the order of the words — “learned” set before “suffered” shows how willingly he entered the school of obedience, obeying in suffering and in dying.

The glorious issue: being made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation to all who obey him. grammatical-historical Barnes guards the phrase “made perfect” — not a moral completing, for he was always sinless, but a vocational one: through sufferings he was made a complete Saviour, the aitios, the procuring cause of salvation. reformed Calvin reads the perfecting as a consecration — his obedient suffering the solemn rite by which he was installed in his priesthood and obtained righteousness for us. And salvation reaches “all who obey”: grammatical-historical Clarke insists this is no bare assent but the obedience of faith, the mark of those who truly receive him.

The chapter’s argument is gathered and sealed: Christ was designated by God a high priest after the order of Melchizedek. grammatical-historical Gill notes the verse returns to v.6, resting Christ’s whole priesthood on his resemblance to that ancient king-priest of Genesis 14 — one without recorded beginning or end, abiding a priest continually. puritan Matthew Henry adds that the writer has “many things to say” of this mysterious figure, but the readers’ dullness will force him to break off and rebuke before he can expound it — which is exactly where the next verses turn.

witnesses.point: Paul builds the whole comparison from the priesthood itself, proving the new covenant’s superiority from first principles, since it had no temple or visible apparatus to display.
The priest must be a true man, for we draw near to God only in one who is himself one of us; and his office is lawful only by God’s call — which Christ waited for rather than seized.
The two marks of a high priest are human sympathy and divine appointment; the wordplay “learned from what he suffered” shows Jesus truly grew in the experience of obedience.
“Learned,” set before “suffered,” shows how willingly Christ entered the school of obedience — obedience in suffering and in dying.

external.name: William L. Lane
external.point: Reads vv.7–10 as the demonstration that the Son qualified for priesthood precisely through his Gethsemane obedience: his prayer was answered not by exemption from death but by his being brought victorious through it.

1.application: The gospel gives you a priest who was appointed, not self-promoting; who prayed with tears in the dark and was answered by being carried through, not around, his death; and who learned, in a body like yours, what obedience costs. Bring him your weakness — he was made perfect in the very place where you are being tested.

11.exposition: The writer has reached the threshold of his richest theme — the Melchizedek priesthood — and there he stops, because his hearers cannot follow. The subject is hard to explain, he says, not for its own obscurity but because they have become dull of hearing. The reproach that follows is stinging: after enough years to be teachers, they need to be re-taught the ABCs of the faith and can stomach only milk (v.12). The one who lives on milk is an infant, unskilled in the word of righteousness; solid food belongs to the mature, whose senses are trained by practice to discern good from evil. It is the letter’s third warning, and its sharpest turn inward: their danger is not open sin but arrested growth, a spiritual sluggishness that leaves them unable to grasp — and so unable to hold fast — the very truths about Christ that would keep them from drifting back.
11.original_language: The charge turns on three words. The readers have become nōthroi (“sluggish, dull”), and the Melchizedek theme is dysermēneutos (“hard to interpret”) — not obscure in itself, but hard to make plain to the slow. What they lack skill in is the logos dikaiosynēs (“word of righteousness”), the fully developed gospel teaching. And maturity is marked by aisthētēria gegymnasmena — faculties “trained by exercise,” the language of the gymnasium, schooled to discern good from evil.
11.historical_context: Milk versus solid food was a stock image for stages of instruction, used by Philo of preliminary studies against advanced knowledge, and by the apostles of the church — by Paul of the Corinthians (1 Corinthians 3:2) and, more warmly, by Peter of the longing for the word (1 Peter 2:2). Gill reckons it now about thirty years from Pentecost — time enough for the readers to have grown into teachers. This is the letter’s third warning, and unlike the others it names an epistemic failure: not open sin but a refusal to grow, which leaves them exposed to the pull back toward the synagogue.
11.christ: The “word of righteousness” the readers cannot yet digest is, in this letter, the deep priestly teaching about the Son — that Jesus is the high priest after Melchizedek’s order, whose single, once-for-all sacrifice accomplished what the law’s repeated offerings never could. Their immaturity is precisely an inability to receive the argument of chapters 7–10. To feed on the solid food is to grasp the supremacy of Christ’s priesthood — and to be anchored by it.

verses.note: Just as the great theme opens, the writer checks himself: of this we have much to say, and it is hard to explain, since you have become dull of hearing. grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the trouble is not that the doctrine is obscure but that their hearing, once acute, has grown sluggish — a lost interest that becomes a lost capacity. grammatical-historical Clarke unpacks nōthroi, “dull”: those who walk heavily and make no speed, always learning yet never coming to the knowledge of the truth. grammatical-historical Barnes adds that the fault lies not in the subject but in the hearers, slow to apprehend the higher things of the gospel.

The rebuke is sharp: by this time you ought to be teachers, yet you need someone to teach you again the basic principles of God’s word — you need milk, not solid food. grammatical-historical Gill lists their advantages — descended from Abraham, heirs of the law and the Scriptures, some having heard Christ and the apostles, and it now about thirty years from Pentecost — so that their backwardness is without excuse. puritan Matthew Henry insists the dullness is no mere natural weakness but a sinful one, the more culpable for the light they had; those who do not improve under the means of grace will inevitably lose ground.

The figure explained: everyone who lives on milk is unskilled in the word of righteousness, for he is an infant. grammatical-historical Robertson renders “unskilled” as apeiros, without experience, and “babe” as nēpios, one not yet able to talk — or to chew. grammatical-historical Ellicott discerns the danger beneath the metaphor: their pull was to blend a rudimentary Christian teaching with the synagogue’s, and by making milk their only food they would forfeit any real knowledge of the gospel and even of the Old Testament itself. To remain on milk by choice is to stand self-confessed a babe.

The bright side of the figure: solid food is for the mature, who by constant use have trained their faculties to discern good from evil. grammatical-historical Barnes explains “by reason of use” as settled habit and practice: the seasoned believer’s spiritual senses have been schooled to tell sound doctrine from error, the wholesome from the harmful. grammatical-historical Gill guards the words “full age” — not a perfection of justification, for babes in Christ are as truly justified as the grown, but a maturity of understanding, fit to feed on the deeper truths. Discernment, then, is the fruit of exercise, not of mere time.

witnesses.point: Their dullness is a sinful, not merely natural, infirmity — the more blameworthy for the advantages they enjoyed; those who fail to improve under grace are already losing ground.
Their hearing, once acute, has grown sluggish; the pull to mingle Christian doctrine with the synagogue’s threatened to rob them of any real knowledge of the gospel.
He reproves an infancy of understanding that forces God, as it were, to prattle with them; babes are unfit for the solid righteousness he is about to unfold.

external.name: George H. Guthrie
external.point: Their arrested growth is itself the danger: only the trained discernment of the mature, formed by long practice, could have guarded them against the attraction of falling back to the old system.

11.application: Spiritual maturity is measured not in years but in exercised discernment. The peril here is not scandalous sin but a quiet drift into dullness — the slow forgetting of what we once knew. Press further into Christ; the muscles of discernment grow only by use.

_meta.title: A High Priest After the Order of Melchizedek
reflection: Christ “learned obedience through what he suffered” and was heard not by escaping death but by being carried through it (vv.7–8). Where are you asking God to remove a trial he may mean to bring you through instead?
Our high priest can “deal gently with the ignorant and wayward” because he shared our weakness (v.2). How does it change the way you approach God to know the one you come to has felt what you feel?

The readers had become “dull of hearing,” needing milk when they should have been teaching (v.12). What deeper truth about Christ have you stopped pressing into — and what would it take to train your discernment again (v.14)?
